text: I love this idea! The closest thing I’ve seen that’s data analysis specific is various “how do do this R thing with pandas” articles helping people transition from 1 ecosystem to another, and the work being done by the data API consortium’s data analysis of 16+ library docs to inform a new standard for dataframe operations : <https://data-apis.org/blog/announcing_the_consortium/|https://data-apis.org/blog/announcing_the_consortium/>

attachments.title: Announcing the Consortium for Python Data API Standards
attachments.text: An initiative to develop API standards for n-dimensional arrays and dataframes

text: I've had some ideas in the back of my head for a while about pushing the abstractions that the hardware to look like the abstractions we need to make fast persistent data structures (immutable.js or clojure's data structures). I'm not a hardware designer by trade, though I've dabbled a bit. Anyone here know if that's a feasible project? I'm not asking for lisp machines btw :slightly_smiling_face:, just wondering if we could make gains by adding hardware functionality for persistent trees.

text: (Sort of a nothingburger pre-announcement video, but I figure a sign of life is a sign of life)

text:
haha, Nodes is far from dead and we are about to release public beta next week. We were just polishing the UI and examples. Unfortunately sh-covid-it happened and i was more focusing on making studio survive this year otherwise we would have launched in spring already.

I’m using it every day. And actually scanned my hdd for all Nodes projects and got: 146 projects 740 graphs and 40k nodes (lots of copy pasted or saved as similar graphs but still).

text:
If you're looking for similar ideas outside data analysis, you might be interested in Eugen Kiss's 7GUIs.
<https://eugenkiss.github.io/7guis/>

>  Whereas in a traditional benchmark competing implementations are compared in terms of their resource consumption, here implementations are compared in terms of their notation. To that end, 7GUIs defines <https://eugenkiss.github.io/7guis/tasks|seven tasks> that represent typical challenges in GUI programming. In addition, 7GUIs provides a recommended <https://eugenkiss.github.io/7guis/dimensions|set of evaluation dimensions>.

text: I realise the benchmarks are still drafts, but I had fun implementing them in R's Tidyverse anyway, which is the most expressive data analysis/wrangling DSL I know. Pic and source attached, should somebody find it useful.

text: I’m working at a data tooling company right now, and I still can’t help but wonder why we keep reinventing the same key data transform metaphors + keep reinventing different versions of tables / spreadsheets etc

text:
An idea for a more elaborate expressiveness task (the largest 7GUIs tasks is pretty non-trivial, too): manually implement a pivot operation in the DSL. This would exercise the DSL nicely, because it involves:

1. add or remove columns
2. remove or add rows
3. move values from/to column names and cells (and sometimes row names, too, if the DSL treats those differently than normal columns)
4. handle missing or duplicate combinations

A pivot_longer example adapted from <https://tidyr.tidyverse.org/articles/pivot.html|Tidyr's pivot vignette>:

> The `family` dataset contains one row per family, and columns `(family, dob_child1, dob_child2, gender_child1, gender_child2)`. Can you, without using your DSL's pivot function, transform this to a table with one row per child, and columns `(family, child, dob, gender)`?
Illustration:
```#> # A tibble: 5 x 5
#>   family dob_child1 dob_child2 gender_child1 gender_child2
#>    <int> <date>     <date>             <int>         <int>
#> 1      1 1998-11-26 2000-01-29             1             2
#> 2      2 1996-06-22 NA                     2            NA
#> 3      3 2002-07-11 2004-04-05             2             2
#> 4      4 2004-10-10 2009-08-27             1             1
#> 5      5 2000-12-05 2005-02-28             2             1

becomes

#> # A tibble: 9 x 4
#>   family child  dob        gender
#>    <int> <chr>  <date>      <int>
#> 1      1 child1 1998-11-26      1
#> 2      1 child2 2000-01-29      2
#> 3      2 child1 1996-06-22      2
#> 4      3 child1 2002-07-11      2
#> 5      3 child2 2004-04-05      2
[...]```

attachments.title: Pivoting
attachments.text: Learn how use the new `pivot_longer()` and `pivot_wider()` functions which change the representation of a dataset without changing the data it contains.

elements.text:
An idea for a more elaborate expressiveness task (the largest 7GUIs tasks is pretty non-trivial, too): manually implement a pivot operation in the DSL. This would exercise the DSL nicely, because it involves:

1. add or remove columns
2. remove or add rows
3. move values from/to column names and cells (and sometimes row names, too, if the DSL treats those differently than normal columns)
4. handle missing or duplicate combinations

A pivot_longer example adapted from
Tidyr's pivot vignette

The `family` dataset contains one row per family, and columns `(family, dob_child1, dob_child2, gender_child1, gender_child2)`. Can you, without using your DSL's pivot function, transform this to a table with one row per child, and columns `(family, child, dob, gender)`?
Illustration:

text:
Another interesting expressiveness dimension is reusing a transformation, i.e. applying a transformation to multiple columns/tables/column-name-schemes. You could ask how DSLs would express

• Apply a transformation to all columns whose name ends with "_date". An interesting one would be the 'add 1 month' transformation where '2020-01-30' becomes '2020-02-28'.
• Apply a transformation to multiple tables, given a list of table names.
• Create a function that creates a column Z = {X - Y when both nonzero; missing value otherwise) --- but the column names X, Y, and Z are parameters, specified by the user at call time. (Harder in DSLs that expect column names as literals, such as SQL or dplyr).

elements.text: Another interesting expressiveness dimension is reusing a transformation, i.e. applying a transformation to multiple columns/tables/column-name-schemes. You could ask how DSLs would express
Apply a transformation to all columns whose name ends with "_date". An interesting one would be the 'add 1 month' transformation where '2020-01-30' becomes '2020-02-28'.
Apply a transformation to multiple tables, given a list of table names.

Create a function that creates a column Z = {X - Y when both nonzero; missing value otherwise) --- but the column names X, Y, and Z are parameters, specified by the user at call time. (Harder in DSLs that expect column names as literals, such as SQL or dplyr).

text: <@U01BSAKUDJQ> those are all great ideas! One weakness in the benchmark is that I don’t have many tasks that really stress the expressiveness of turing-incomplete langs like SQL/datalog. I like “transform all columns with _date in the name” because there’s a level of reflection required that I think SQL/datalog don’t have.

text:
<@UAJKEBGP8> more broadly, I’m searching for a taxonomy of tabular analytics tasks. I didn’t have a particular method for selecting tasks. I just picked what seemed common to my personal experience. But I’d like a more systematic way of picking tasks that cover the space.

For example, this paper is a good start: <https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/1532136>
